coredata.dc:title: Use of Adaptive Focused Acoustics™ ultrasound in controlling liposome formation
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Katherine C.
Kakumanu, Srikanth
Beckett, Carl D.
Laugharn, James A.
coredata.dc:description: Many techniques for producing large unilamellar vesicles (LUVs) or small unilamellar vesicles (SUVs) have drawbacks, including exposure of sensitive biological materials to harsh organic solvents or high temperatures. Here we describe the use of controlled focused ultrasound, Adaptive Focused Acoustics™ (AFA), to make LUV or SUV at low temperature without organic solvents and at a consistent, chosen size. We studied the effects of peak incident power (PIP), cycles per burst (CPB), duty factor (DF), temperature, and lipid composition (natural or synthetic), on liposome size distribution. We found that an increase in PIP, DF, CPB, or temperature decreased liposome size. When processed under the same conditions as the natural lipid composition [Phospholipon 90 G], the synthetic lipid composition [HSPC, DSPE-PEG-2000, Chol] generally produced larger liposomes, although extending processing time reduced liposomes to similar size. In combination with AFA, these trends can help pinpoint parameter values that achieve a desired liposome size distribution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Unilamellar liposomes
Adaptive Focused Acoustics
Focused ultrasound
Vesicle
Sonication

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Generation of unilamellar vesicles from acoustic energy
Generation of unilamellar vesicles from acoustic energy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the acoustic energy emitted by the AFA instrument, showing the small focal zone in the sample vessel. A water bath keeps the process isothermal.
Structures of the natural and synthetic phospholipids used, showing the hydrophilic heads and hydrophobic tails.

(Left) EM image of a partially processed 2mL sample of natural lipid formulation [Phospholipon 90 G] subjected to AFA at 3°C with PIP=75W, DF=50%, CPB=1000 and t =12min. (Right) EM image of a natural lipid formulation processed under identical conditions, showing a larger number of liposomes.

Dependence of Z av (nm) (Ia) or PDI (Ib) on PIP (W) during AFA at 3°C with 2mL samples of natural lipid formulation [Phospholipon 90 G] at CPB=1000, DF=50%, and t =2min. At 350W, the error bars are not visible because the variance is very small. Dependence of Z av (nm) (IIa) or PDI (IIb) on PIP (W) during AFA at 10°C with 2mL samples of synthetic lipid formulation [HSPC, DSPE-PEG-2000, Chol] at CPB=1000, DF=50%, and t =2h. Each data point for both formulations is the average of n =4 samples measured 3 times each, for a total of 12 measurements per point.

Bimodal distribution of liposomes made from natural lipid Z av (nm), shifting and narrowing towards a small-sized unimodal distribution with higher PIP. AFA parameters were as described in Table 1 for PIP.

Dependence of Z av (nm) (Ia) or PDI (Ib) on CPB during AFA at 3°C with 2mL samples of natural lipid formulation [Phospholipon 90 G] and PIP=150W, DF=50%, and t =2min. Dependence of Z av (nm) (IIa) or PDI (IIb) on CPB during AFA at 10°C with 2mL samples of synthetic lipid formulation [HSPC, DSPE-PEG-2000, Chol] and PIP=150W, DF=50%, and t =2h. Each data point for both formulations is the average of n =4 samples measured 3 times each, for a total of 12 measurements per point.

Dependence of Z av (nm) (Ia) or PDI (Ib) on CPB and temperature during AFA with 2mL samples of natural lipid formulation [Phospholipon 90 G] and PIP=150W, DF=50%, and t =2min. The chiller was set to 3°C, 10°C, or 20°C. Warmer regions (red, orange) indicate higher values of Z av or PDI and cooler regions (green, blue) indicate lower values of Z av or PDI. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Dependence of Z av (nm) (Ia) or PDI (Ib) on DF during AFA at 3°C with 2mL samples of natural lipid formulation [Phospholipon 90 G] and PIP=150W, CPB=1000, and t =2min. Each data point for the natural lipid formulation is the average of n =4 samples measured 3 times each, for a total of 12 measurements per point. (II) Dependence of size (nm) on DF during AFA at 10°C with 2mL samples of synthetic lipid formulation [HSPC, DSPE-PEG-2000, Chol] and PIP=150W, CPB=1000, and t =2h. This graph uses measurements from the Horiba laser diffraction instrument, which gives a mean size instead of Z av. Each data point for the synthetic lipid formulation is the average of n =4 samples measured once each.
Expected assembly of phospholipids into unilamellar liposome. Hydrophobic tails of the lipid molecules orient to form an inner layer.

Parameters used for each substudy. Multiple items in one cell indicate a parameter varied for that substudy. The items in a list correspond in order to those of a list in the same row. For instance, the first substudy in the table varied PIP and duration. So the experiment was run with 50W at 360s, 75W at 240s, and so on in accordance with the table.

head.ce:title: Use of Adaptive Focused Acoustics™ ultrasound in controlling liposome formation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, 77 Massachusetts Avenue, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Covaris Process Technologies, Covaris Inc., 14 Gill St. Unit H, Woburn, MA 01801, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Many techniques for producing large unilamellar vesicles (LUVs) or small unilamellar vesicles (SUVs) have drawbacks, including exposure of sensitive biological materials to harsh organic solvents or high temperatures. Here we describe the use of controlled focused ultrasound, Adaptive Focused Acoustics™ (AFA), to make LUV or SUV at low temperature without organic solvents and at a consistent, chosen size. We studied the effects of peak incident power (PIP), cycles per burst (CPB), duty factor (DF), temperature, and lipid composition (natural or synthetic), on liposome size distribution. We found that an increase in PIP, DF, CPB, or temperature decreased liposome size. When processed under the same conditions as the natural lipid composition [Phospholipon 90 G], the synthetic lipid composition [HSPC, DSPE-PEG-2000, Chol] generally produced larger liposomes, although extending processing time reduced liposomes to similar size. In combination with AFA, these trends can help pinpoint parameter values that achieve a desired liposome size distribution.

ce:keyword.ce:text: critical radius described by Winterhalter and Lasic [20]
critical radius described by Richardson et al. [24]

ce:section.ce:para: A wealth of literature has been written on the use of liposomes in drug delivery, indicating that it is a rapidly growing field [1–8]. Work on liposomes as a drug delivery mechanism has focused on producing three types of liposomes: multilamellar vesicles (MLV), large unilamellar vesicles (LUV), and small unilamellar vesicles (SUV). LUV range from 0.1 to 1μm in diameter, and SUV range from 20 to 100nm in diameter [7,9,10]. Depending on the size and properties of the material to be encapsulated, LUV and SUV are often preferred over MLV as drug carriers. LUV and SUV are prepared by first manufacturing MLV then breaking them into LUV or SUV [11].

Conventional liposome preparation methods include detergent depletion, ethanol/ether injection, reverse-phase evaporation, and demulsification methods [6,7,10,12]. More advanced methods include freeze drying of a monophase solution, heating, supercritical fluid injection and decompression, and the use of a membrane contactor [7,13]. Processing methods for making liposomes include high-pressure homogenization, extrusion, and conventional ultrasound [14]. These methods all have one or more of the following disadvantages: (i) usage of large amounts of organic solvents; (ii) uneven and high temperatures leading to possible degradation of the samples from heat exposure; (iii) high pressure; (iv) sterilization issues; (v) loss of material; and/or (vi) a lengthy time requirement [7,13–15].

Ultrasound is a proven method of preparing liposomes, with a long history [10]. However, because traditional ultrasound is unfocused and has a low frequency, energy conversion is inefficient, and effective process control cannot be achieved [14,15]. In contrast, Adaptive Focused Acoustics™ (AFA) (Covaris, Inc.), uses focused ultrasound. Previous studies investigating the use of AFA in making liposomes found that the technology was highly effective in producing liposomes with characteristics similar to those produced by current industry methods, including bath sonication followed by extrusion [16]. AFA-produced liposomes were shown to be unilamellar and the size of the liposomes were highly reproducible from batch to batch. This technology does not require the use of organic solvent, and keeps the material being processed at isothermal conditions, which is particularly important for sensitive biological molecules. AFA’s precise control of energy at a focal point can be used to regulate the amount of shear stress present and to limit the generation of biologically damaging free radicals. Furthermore, AFA does not require high temperatures or pressures to successfully operate [14,15].

The AFA sonicator used in this study operates at a frequency of about 500kHz, approximately 20 to 40 times higher than traditional sonicators. Due to the short wavelength (∼2mm) used in AFA, the ultrasonic acoustic energy can be focused into a discrete zone in the processing vessel. With AFA, emitted ultrasound is focused using a concave transducer (Fig. 1 ), converging the acoustic energy to a precise point inside a processing vessel without direct contact, preventing cross-contamination. The processing vessel is loaded with liposome-forming materials and aqueous buffers and exposed to an active acoustic focal point, permitting control of cavitation and streaming energy to form liposomes with particular size characteristics. Isothermal processing conditions are maintained by immersion in a water bath, which is connected to a chiller controlling the water temperature. Several parameters can be independently programmed on the AFA sonicators in order to precisely adjust the characteristics of the ultrasound bursts [14,17,18].

The emitted ultrasound provides the external energy required to induce curvature in the lipid and consequent formation of liposomes [19]. Two methods have been proposed to induce curvature: (i) breaking up lipid to form phospholipid bilayer fragments (PBFs), whose hydrophobic part is exposed to the hydrophilic environment, driving the fragments to minimize that unfavorable edge energy by curving up into liposomes, and (ii) changing solubility conditions [20]. Of these, the first mechanism is more prevalent in literature [9,20,21].

PBFs are formed through bubble cavitation induced by ultrasound waves, as described in the Rayleigh-Plesset, Gilmore, and Keller-Miksis models [22]. Cavitation bubbles grow from tiny contaminants in the surrounding liquid, and increase in size with the accumulation of dissolved gases like oxygen from the surrounding liquid, over several cycles. From here, PBFs may be formed in two ways: collapse cavitation and stable cavitation. In collapse cavitation, the bubble grows until a critical (resonant) size is reached, then it grows extremely rapidly until it cannot accumulate gases as efficiently as before, and collapses [23]. In stable cavitation, the bubble grows to a relatively stable point, oscillating in size, but not collapsing. Because the presence of collapse cavitation has not been shown to correlate with mean liposome size [24], stable cavitation most likely has a major role in forming PBFs.

The stabilized, oscillating bubble produces radiation pressure, which attracts lipid toward the source of the vibrations because the lipid is denser than the surrounding medium [24,25]. Acoustic microstreaming around the bubble provides shear forces that break apart the lipids into PBFs. If the PBF is above a critical radius (CR), as described by Winterhalter and Lasic, then it will follow the energetically favorable path and curve up into a liposome [20]. If not, it will fuse with other PBFs until it reaches a CR, whereupon it will then curve into a liposome [9,26].

Because the shear force around the stable cavitation bubbles largely determines the size of the formed PBFs and consequently liposome size, it is advantageous to know what parameters will yield the correct application of shear force to give the desired liposome size. Such parameters include density, pressure, surface tension, temperature and viscosity of the surrounding liquid, amount and characteristics of dissolved gases, and the duration and manner of ultrasonic energy input [21]. The AFA instrument has very precise control over parameters such as peak incident power (PIP), cycles per burst (CPB), duty factor (DF), and duration (t), which are detailed below.

The power in watts emitted from the transducer during the active period of one transducer course. A transducer course consists of a period where the transducer is active and emitting energy and a relaxation period in which it is inactive. This parameter controls the amplitude of the acoustic oscillations and has a maximum value of 500W with the AFA instrument.
The number of acoustic oscillations contained in the active period of one transducer course. The sonicator used in this study has a maximum CPB of 1000.
The percentage of time in one transducer course when the transducer is actively emitting acoustic energy. The sonicator used in this study has a maximum DF of 50%.

The time for which the sample is subjected to AFA treatment. As previously stated, this was shown to be correlated with a decrease in the average size (Z av) of the liposome product [21,26,27]. Z av as used in our experiments is a volume-averaged particle size.
In this study, we examine the impact of various AFA process parameters on the formed PBFs and resulting liposome size distribution.

Phosphatidylcholine (Phospholipon 90 G) and cholesterol (Chol) were purchased from Lipoid GmbH (Ludwishafen, Germany) and Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO), respectively. 1,2-distearoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoethanolzmine-N-[methoxy(polyethyelene glycol)-2000] (DSPE-PEG-2000) and hydrogenated (soy) phosphocholine (HSPC) were purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids (Alabaster, AL). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS; 0.1M and pH of 7.4) was made from chemicals of analytical grade from standard sources. All materials were used as purchased. The structures of Phospholipon 90 G, DSPE-PEG-2000, and HSPC are shown in Fig. 2 .

Lipid compositions were determined with a high precision microbalance (Sartorius). For preparing liposomes from natural lipid, 20mg of Phospholipon 90 G in 2mL of deionized water was used. For preparing liposomes from synthetic lipids, 11.4mg of HSPC, 3.8mg of DSPE-PEG-2000, and 4.2mg of Chol in 2mL of 0.1M PBS was used. Samples of these two lipid compositions were placed in 2mL vessels (12×24mm, Covaris, Inc., Woburn, MA) to undergo ultrasound treatment from a commercial AFA sonicator (S220x, Covaris, Inc.) under the conditions described in Table 1 . The solid liposome components were placed in the vessel first, followed by the aqueous media. Dispersion of lipids directly into the aqueous media was performed entirely by the AFA sonicator.

We studied the effects of DF, CPB, PIP, and temperature on the formation of liposomes from both synthetic and natural lipid compositions. Initial conditions were determined from previous studies (50% DF, 1000 CPB, 150W PIP) [14,15]. The initial duration and temperature for the synthetic and natural compositions were 1h at 10.0°C and 2min at 3.0°C, respectively. When studying the effect of CPB using a synthetic composition, 2h was used for the duration. Table 1 describes the parameter values for each experiment.

Because the breakup of the lipid requires external energy, merely increasing energy input would decrease Z av. Therefore, when studying the effects of DF and PIP, which affect the amount of energy input, care was taken to appropriately vary the duration of the experiment such that the total amount of energy delivered to the sample remained constant. The total energy delivered may be calculated as Energy=(PIP*DF)* t, and is displayed in Table 1.

The average hydrodynamic particle diameters (Z av) and polydispersity indices (PDI) of liposomes were determined by dynamic light scattering at 25°C (Zetasizer Nano ZS-90, Malvern Instruments Ltd., U.K.). This instrument uses a Brownian motion model to measure particle size, using dynamic light scattering to find the speed of the particles and relating those diffusion coefficients to sizes through the Stokes-Einstein equation. The instrument has a measurement detection range of 2nm to 3μm [28]. Three independent measurements were taken by the instrument for every sample.

When the particle diameters fell outside the range that could be reliably determined through dynamic light scattering, size determination was supplemented using laser diffraction (Partica LA-950, Horiba Scientific, Japan). This instrument has a measurement detection range of 0.01 to 3000μm, but does not measure the PDI [29].

The ff EM was done by Nano Analytical Laboratory (San Francisco, CA). Liposome samples were cryofixed at a cooling rate of 10,000°C. Formation of ice crystals was minimized by quenching the liposome samples with liquid nitrogen-cooled propane and the sandwich technique. Cryofixed samples were fractured on JEOL JED-9000 freeze etching equipment, then the exposed fracture planes were shadowed with platinum for 30s in an angle of 25–35° and with carbon for 35s (2kV/60–70mA, 1×10−5 torr). Produced replicas were cleaned with concentrated nitric acid for 24h, then agitated with chloroform a minimum of 5 times. Replicas were inspected with a JEOL 100 CX electron microscope, and micrograph images taken.

A total of 12 measurements were taken per set of preparation conditions (4 samples, 3 measurements each) and averaged. Error bars represent the standard deviation of these measurements. Horiba measurements: The “mean size” of the 4 samples in a set was averaged. Error bars represent the standard deviation of these averages.
The software MATLAB R2013a (MathWorks, MA) was used to generate the graphs. The MATLAB function griddata was used to interpolate between data points from experimental results.

The AFA instrument has four main parameters that affect the amount and manner of acoustic energy transmitted to the sample: DF, CPB, PIP, and duration. The Z av is additionally dependent on parameters such as temperature of the AFA water bath and lipid composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of unilamellar vesicles from acoustic energy
ce:section.ce:para: A sample of the natural lipid composition was partially processed in order to examine the liposome formation process. The left electron microscopy image in Fig. 3 shows a large piece of lipid, fragmenting on its edges. The breakup appears to be fairly uniform, particularly toward the top of the image, generating fragments with small variances in size. The right electron microscopy image in Fig. 3 shows the formation of a number of nanosized liposomes.

The effect of the temperature of the surrounding liquid was studied simultaneously with the effect of CPB using a natural lipid composition (the data presented in Fig. 7 is taken from a different experiment than those in Fig. 6). Although both temperature and CPB data are presented, the independent effect of temperature may be accurately inferred because the trends in CPB are parallel at each temperature. Fig. 7 shows a decrease in Z av with increasing temperature. The PDI decreased from 3 to 10°C, but increased at 20°C for most values of CPB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of unilamellar vesicles from acoustic energy
ce:section.ce:para: Literature on AFA reports that the technology forms either LUVs or SUVs as the end product [14,21]. Accordingly, it is expected that the component phospholipids will assemble in a unilamellar layer, as shown in Fig. 9 . The unilamellar vesicles are formed by breakup of MLVs, rather than being generated directly, a process which appears to be reflected in Fig. 3. The large piece of lipid in the left image is likely a MLV fragmenting into PBFs, liposome precursors. The nanosized liposomes in the right image are likely mostly SUVs with a few LUVs.

The decrease in Z av with increasing PIP may reflect the convergence to a critical radius and bimodal size distribution described by Richardson, et al. [24]. Note that this critical radius differs from that previously discussed; the CR described by Winterhalter and Lasic [20] refers to the minimum radius required for a PBF to curl into a liposome, while the critical radius described by Richardson, et al. [24] refers to the size at which a liposome will not be further sheared to a smaller size under the given processing conditions. From this point forward, the latter will be denoted as “RCR” (Richardson’s critical radius). Lipids with a radius larger than RCR undergo strong shear forces that break them up into PBFs, which then form into smaller liposomes. Thus we expect that as the permitted size range decreases with increasing PIP, liposome size would converge to RCR. The bimodal distribution and peak shift should follow the trends of (i) increasing area under the peak below RCR and (ii) decreasing area under the peak above RCR, and that is indeed what we observed when examining peak distribution data (Fig. 5).

The decrease in Z av with increased CPB (Fig. 6) may reflect an increase in the radius of the stable cavitation bubbles with increased CPB, bubbles responsible for the shear force that generated the PBFs. Supposing that bubble sizes were larger, more lipid could come into contact with the stronger shear forces at the bubble interface. This would have had the effect of processing more lipids, producing more PBFs, and being able to shear larger pieces that a smaller sized bubble would not have been able to completely break up. We propose that consistent with our observations, the PBF radii became smaller with increasing CPB, and so did the resulting liposomes.

These findings appear different from those of Tejera-Garcia, et al., who found a concave trend in Z av with CPB [21]. This difference may be due to the older sonicator used (S2, Covaris, Inc.), as the S2 used a dimensionless parameter called “intensity”. Unlike its current counterpart PIP, using the same intensity with different values of CPB would change the amount of energy imparted to the sample. Higher intensity and lower CPB values correspond to higher values of PIP [30]. Thus, as CPB was increased, the energy imparted to the sample decreased and so the effect of CPB could not be measured independently. Indeed, our study showed that a decrease in PIP or DF (less energy imparted) produced larger liposome sizes, in agreement with the findings of Tejera-Garcia et al. [21] using higher CPB values where less energy was imparted.

Notably, the synthetic composition appeared to reach RCR at a much lower CPB than the natural composition (200 CPB and 750 CPB, respectively). This difference may be due to natural lipids’ polyunsaturated fatty acids, making it inherently less stable relative to the lipids in the synthetic composition [31]. It was previously proposed that when CPB and thus cavitation bubble radius and shear force increased, more PBFs were formed. These PBFs have some variation in size and shape; perhaps Phospholipon 90 G breaks up in a more irregular fashion due to its inherent lesser stability, and the synthetic composition breaks up along more regular lines. This variation would become less significant as CPB increases, since there are more PBFs overall.

The decrease in Z av with increasing temperature (Fig. 7) was likely due to the additional energy the increased temperature provides, energy that no longer needs to be provided by the sonicator. Although higher temperature allows even smaller liposomes to be formed, by adjusting CPB upward small sized liposomes can still be obtained even at low temperatures.

The observed decrease in Z av with increased DF (Fig. 8) is likely due to two factors. First, increasing DF resulted in longer sonication durations per burst, although the total time of sonication per sample remained constant for all DF values in our study. It may be that this longer sonication gave shear forces time to not only form PBFs, but also break them into even smaller sized PBFs. Additionally, increased DF resulted in more overall energy per burst transmitted to the sample, possibly providing the additional external energy required to break up more lipid. Second, we propose that the DF parameter, which indicates the relaxation time between acoustic bursts, only allowed PBFs of a “permitted” size range enough time to curl up into liposomes during the transducer inactive period, with the lower limit of that range being the CR discussed earlier. Perhaps the PBFs above that range did not have enough time to completely curl up before the next acoustic burst, and PBFs smaller than that size range did not have enough time to fuse together to achieve the necessary size to curl up prior to the next burst. As DF increased, the relaxation time decreased, and the upper limit of that size range decreased, while the lower limit remained unchanged. After the final burst of acoustic energy, it is likely that the PBFs below CR fused and then curled up into liposomes with sizes that fall within the certain size range determined by DF.

Trends for both compositions were similar to that exhibited by the effect of PIP, which is consistent with the relationship between PIP and DF in determining the energy imparted to the sample (Energy=(PIP*DF)* t). Rearranging the equation to Energy/t =(PIP*DF), we see that an increase in either PIP or DF will increase the energy imparted per time, which we call power. Thus, it is reasonable that PIP and DF have the same effects on Z av and PDI, though the mechanism of action differs. With increasing DF, as previously described, a longer sonication time per burst and thus more overall energy imparted over the interval provides more opportunities to break up lipid, while with increasing PIP, more energy is imparted over the same duration because the amplitude of the ultrasound wave has increased. Essentially, increasing DF increases both Energy and t, although the Energy term increases more, while with PIP, only Energy increases.

Traditional methods to prepare LUV or SUV have certain disadvantages that AFA surmounts. The impact of various AFA parameters on liposome formation was studied: DF, CPB, and PIP, as well as the temperature of the surrounding liquid and the lipid composition. The DF and PIP parameters affected the power input of the sonicator, and the temperature affected the total energy of the sample. A higher power decreased liposome size but increased its polydispersity, while higher overall energy decreased both size and polydispersity. CPB regulated the size of the stable cavitation bubbles responsible for liposome formation, thus controlling size and polydispersity. The synthetic composition was generally found to yield larger liposomes than the natural lipid under the same processing conditions, although extending the processing time could significantly reduce the size of the synthetic composition. In conclusion, the described trends of DF, PIP, CPB, temperature of surrounding liquid, and lipid composition could be used to quickly iterate to a desired, precise and reproducible liposome size using AFA.
No funding from institutions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank X. He and R. Jain for reviewing and supporting the research.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances with liposomes as pharmaceutical carriers
Liposomal formulations of anticancer drugs: selectivity and effectiveness
Novel procedures for generating and loading liposomal systems

Development of liposomal amphotericin B formulation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conventional and dense gas techniques for the production of liposomes: a review
Freeze fracture electron microscopic (ff EM) examination & analysis of liposomes produced by Covaris AFA Technology
Focused ultrasound – a novel tool for liposome formulation
Oxidized phosphatidylcholines facilitate phospholipid flip-flop in liposomes
Peptide-mediated targeting of liposomes to TrkB receptor-expressing cells
Internalization of liposome nanoparticles functionalized with TrkB ligand in rat cochlear cell populations
Liposome stability and formation: experimental parameters and theories on the size distribution
Making unilamellar liposomes using focused ultrasound
The role of cavitation in liposome formation
Mechanisms for nonthermal effects of sound
Effect of ultrasound parameters for unilamellar liposome preparation
Effects of frequency and power of ultrasound on the size reduction of liposome

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Zetasizer Nano Series User Manual, 2004. University of Cambridge Department of Biochemistry Web site. <http://www.biophysics.bioc.cam.ac.uk/files/Zetasizer_Nano_ user_manual_Man0317-1.1.pdf>, 2004 (accessed 26.03.15).
